name: Sweet Corn

water_guidance.underwater_signs: Curling, greyish-green leaves during hot afternoons.
water_guidance.overwater_signs: Yellowing lower leaves, shallow weak roots.

good.note: Beans fix nitrogen in soil corn depletes heavily.
Squash's broad leaves shade the soil and suppress weeds around the base of the corn.

bad.explanation: Both attract corn earworm/tomato fruitworm — same pest, same species.
bad.mitigation: Not toxic to each other. Fine if space-constrained, just monitor both closely for the shared pest.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Competes for light and airflow; increases disease from poor ventilation; stunted ear development.
spacing_consequences.too_far: Wastes bed space and weakens wind pollination since corn relies on nearby plants for pollen transfer.

notes: Plant in blocks, not rows — wind pollination needs it. Heavy nitrogen feeder, classic Three Sisters companion with beans and squash.

name: Pole Beans

water_guidance.underwater_signs: Wilting leaves in afternoon heat; dry, curling pods.
water_guidance.overwater_signs: Yellowing leaves, root rot, poor pod set.

good.note: Climbs the corn stalk instead of needing a built trellis — the classic Three Sisters pairing.
Nitrogen fixed by the beans benefits the shallow-feeding carrots planted nearby or after.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Poor airflow raises fungal disease risk (rust, powdery mildew) and reduces pod set.
spacing_consequences.too_far: Wastes trellis space; weeds fill the gaps.

notes: Needs a trellis or tall companion (like corn) to climb. Fixes nitrogen in soil — good predecessor for heavy feeders like corn or kale.

name: Snap Peas

water_guidance.overwater_signs: Yellowing leaves, powdery mildew, root rot.

good.note: Peas mature and get pulled early, so they don't compete with carrots for space, and leave nitrogen behind.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Crowded vines get poor airflow, raising powdery mildew risk.

notes: Cool-season crop — sow several weeks BEFORE the last frost, not after (note the negative direct_sow_after_last_frost_days). Stops producing once summer heat sets in.

name: Tomato

water_guidance.underwater_signs: Afternoon leaf curl/wilting; blossom end rot from inconsistent watering.
water_guidance.overwater_signs: Cracked fruit, yellowing lower leaves, fungal leaf spot.

good.note: Tomato foliage shades the soil for carrots; carrots aerate soil around tomato roots.
Fast, shallow-rooted lettuce works as living ground cover under taller tomato plants without competing much.

bad.explanation: Both attract corn earworm/tomato fruitworm — same pest, same species.
bad.mitigation: Not toxic to each other. Fine if space-constrained, just monitor both closely for the shared pest.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Poor airflow raises early blight and other fungal disease risk; plants compete for light.

notes: Stake or cage early so you don't disturb roots later. Avoid planting where other nightshades (pepper, potato) grew the previous year — shared soil-borne disease risk.

name: Bell Pepper

good.note: Low, shallow-rooted carrots don't compete much with pepper's root zone.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Poor airflow raises fungal disease risk; plants shade each other's fruit set.

notes: Slower to establish than tomato — start indoors early. Fruit color changes as it ripens (green to red/yellow/orange depending on variety); flavor sweetens the longer it's left on the plant.

name: Summer Squash

water_guidance.underwater_signs: Midday wilting; small or bitter fruit.

good.note: Broad leaves shade the soil around corn, suppressing weeds and retaining moisture.
Benefits from the nitrogen beans leave behind.

bad.explanation: Same family (Cucurbitaceae) — cucumber beetles move freely between them and can vector bacterial wilt to both.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Poor airflow raises powdery mildew risk; sprawling vines compete hard for space.
spacing_consequences.too_far: Wastes bed space early in the season before vines fill in.

notes: Prolific once established — two or three plants usually cover a household. Harvest young and small for the best texture; oversized fruit turns woody.

name: Cucumber

good.note: Benefits from nitrogen beans leave behind.

bad.explanation: Same family (Cucurbitaceae) — cucumber beetles move freely between them and can vector bacterial wilt to both.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Poor airflow raises powdery mildew risk; crowding deforms fruit.

notes: Trellising keeps fruit straighter and improves airflow compared to letting vines sprawl on the ground.

name: Lettuce

water_guidance.overwater_signs: Mushy leaves, rot at the base.

good.note: Shallow lettuce roots and deep carrot roots don't compete much, and lettuce matures much faster so it's out of the way before carrots need the space.
Grows well in the partial shade cast by taller tomato plants, especially once summer heats up.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Stunted heads and poor air circulation invites rot.

notes: Bolts (turns bitter, goes to seed) in summer heat — best as a spring/fall crop, or grown in partial shade during hot months. Fast enough to succession-plant every couple of weeks for a steady supply.

name: Kale

good.note: Benefits from nitrogen beans leave behind — kale is a heavy nitrogen feeder.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Stunted leaves and poor airflow invites fungal issues.

notes: Cut-and-come-again — harvest outer leaves and the plant keeps producing from the center. Flavor improves after a light frost. Rotate brassica beds yearly to avoid clubroot buildup.

name: Carrot

water_guidance.underwater_signs: Stunted, woody roots; splitting if heavily watered after a dry spell.

good.note: Benefits from nitrogen beans leave behind.
Peas mature and clear out early, leaving nitrogen and space behind.
Tomato foliage shades the soil; carrots aerate soil around tomato roots.
Shallow lettuce roots and deep carrot roots don't compete much.

spacing_consequences.too_close: Crowding stunts or forks the roots.

notes: Needs loose, rock-free soil to grow straight roots — heavy clay or compacted soil causes stunted or forked carrots. Avoid fresh manure or high-nitrogen amendments right before planting; excess nitrogen causes hairy, forked roots.
